sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 8 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 77, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 76 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The DOWN movement behaved unexpectedly:
- DOWN 1: left stayed at (19,19), right moved from (39,44) to (44,44) - right moved down
- DOWN 2: left stayed at (19,19), right moved from (44,44) to (49,44) - right moved down
- DOWN 3: left stayed at (19,19), right moved from (49,44) to (54,44) - right moved down
- DOWN 4: no change - right blocked at row 54
Wait, that's strange. DOWN moved the right square DOWN (row increased from 39 to 54), but the left square stayed at row 19. This confirms that each square moves independently within its chamber.
But wait - I expected DOWN to move both squares down. But the left square stayed at row 19 while the right square moved from 39 to 54. This means the left square is already at its bottom-most position? No, that doesn't make sense because earlier the left square was at row 44 and could move up to row 19.
Actually, let me reconsider. Earlier:
- Initial: both squares at row 44
- After 4x UP: left at row 29, right at row 39 (both moved up)
- After 5th UP: left at row 24, right stayed at row 39 (right blocked)
- After 6th UP: left at row 19, right stayed at row 39 (right still blocked)
So the left square can move from row 19 to row 44 (and possibly further), and the right square can move from row 39 to row 54.
Wait, but the left square was at row 44 initially and moved up to row 19. So its vertical range is 19 to 44 (or possibly further down). And the right square was at row 44 initially, moved up to row 39 (blocked), and down to row 54 (blocked). So its vertical range is 39 to 54.
But when I did DOWN from the current position (left at 19, right at 44), the left square stayed at 19 while the right moved from 44 to 54. This means the left square is blocked from moving down at row 19? That seems odd.
Wait, let me re-read the results:
- DOWN 1: left=(19, 19), right=(44, 44) - but before this, left was at (19,19) and right was at (39,44). So right moved from 39 to 44, left stayed at 19.
- DOWN 2: left=(19, 19), right=(49, 44) - wait, right moved from col 44 to col 49? No, the format is (row, col), so right moved from (44, 44) to (49, 44). Row increased from 44 to 49, col stayed at 44.
- DOWN 3: left=(19, 19), right=(54, 44) - right moved from (49, 44) to (54, 44). Row increased from 49 to 54.
So the right square's vertical range is 39 to 54 (in steps of 5).
And the left square stayed at row 19 throughout all DOWN moves. This means the left square is blocked from moving down at row 19.
But earlier, the left square was at row 44 and moved up to row 19. So how can it be blocked from moving down at row 19?
Oh wait, I think I misunderstood the movement. Let me reconsider:
- UP moves the square that is LOWER (higher row number) UP toward the other square
- DOWN moves the square that is HIGHER (lower row number) DOWN toward the other square
No wait, that doesn't match the earlier observations either.
Let me re-examine:
- Initial: both at row 44
- UP: both moved to row 39 (both up)
- UP: both moved to row 34 (both up)
- UP: both moved to row 29 (both up)
- UP: left moved to row 24, right stayed at row 39 (right blocked)
- UP: left moved to row 19, right stayed at row 39 (right still blocked)
So UP moves both squares up, but each is blocked by its own chamber's top wall.
Then from (left=19, right=39):
- DOWN: left stayed at 19, right moved to 44
- DOWN: left stayed at 19, right moved to 49
- DOWN: left stayed at 19, right moved to 54
So DOWN moves both squares down, but the left square is blocked at row 19 from moving down? That's strange because row 19 is higher (smaller) than row 39, so the left square is above the right square.
Wait, I think I'm confusing myself. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, I think the movement semantics might be:
- UP: moves each square up by 5, but blocked by chamber walls
- DOWN: moves each square down by 5, but blocked by chamber walls
- LEFT: moves left square left by 5, right square right by 5
- RIGHT: moves left square right by 5, right square left by 5
But the left square is at row 19 and didn't move down. This could mean the left square's chamber has a floor at row 19 (or the square is blocked by something else).
Wait, but earlier the left square was at row 44 and moved up to row 19. So row 19 is the TOP of the left chamber, not the bottom. If the left square is at the top and tries to move down, it should be able to move down... unless the chamber has a different shape.
Hmm, let me look at the ascii output from earlier:
Left chamber area (rows 15-25, cols 10-25):
  15: YYYYBBBBBBBBBBBB
  16: YYYYBBBBBBBBBBBB
  17: YYYYBBBBBBBBBBBB
  18: YYYYBBBBBBBBBBBB
  19: BBBBBBBBBSSSSSYY
  20: BBBBBBBBBSSSSSYY
  21: BBBBBBBBBSSSSSYY
  22: BBBBBBBBBSSSSSYY
  23: BBBBBBBBBSSSSSYY
  24: BBBBBBBBBYYYYYYY
  25: BBBBBBBBBYYYYYYY
So at rows 19-23, the left square occupies cols 19-23 (SSSSS). Below at rows 24-25, there's yellow (YYYYYYY), which means the chamber continues downward. But the square didn't move down from row 19...
Oh wait, I think I see the issue. The left square is at col 19-23, and the chamber below might be blocked by walls. Let me look more carefully at the ascii.
Actually, looking at the ascii, rows 24-25 show BBBBBBBBBYYYYYYY, which means cols 10-18 are black (walls) and cols 19-25 are yellow (open). So the chamber does continue downward from the square's position.
But the square didn't move down. This is confusing. Let me reconsider the movement semantics.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the DOWN results more carefully:
- Before DOWN: left at (19, 19), right at (39, 44)
- DOWN 1: left at (19, 19), right at (44, 44)
Hmm, the right square moved from (39, 44) to (44, 44). Row increased from 39 to 44, col stayed at 44. So the right square moved down by 5 rows.
But the left square stayed at (19, 19). This means the left square is blocked from moving down.
But why? The chamber seems to continue downward...
Oh! I think I understand now. Maybe the movement is not "both squares move in the same direction" but rather "the squares move toward/away from each other."
Let me reconsider:
- When both squares are at the same row (44), UP moves both up (they move together)
- When squares are at different rows (19 and 39), UP moves the lower one (row 39) up... but no, the left square at row 19 moved up to row 19 (stayed), and the right square at row 39 stayed.
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me re-examine the earlier UP moves:
- After 4x UP: left at row 29, right at row 39
- After 5th UP: left at row 24, right at row 39
So the left square (at row 29) moved up to row 24, while the right square (at row 39) stayed. This means UP moved the square that was at the LOWER row number (higher position) even higher up. The right square at row 39 was blocked.
Then:
- After 6th UP: left at row 19, right at row 39
- After DOWN 1: left at row 19, right at row 44
So DOWN moved the right square from row 39 to row 44 (down), but the left square stayed at row 19. This means DOWN moves the square that is at the HIGHER row number (lower position) even lower. The left square at row 19 was... blocked? Or maybe DOWN only moves the lower square?
Wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the semantics are:
- UP: moves the HIGHER square (lower row number) up by 5
- DOWN: moves the LOWER square (higher row number) down by 5
- LEFT: moves left square left by 5, right square right by 5
- RIGHT: moves left square right by 5, right square left by 5
Let me check:
- Both at row 44: UP moves both up to 39. This works if both are considered "higher" (same level).
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP moves left (at 29, higher) to 24. Right at 39 is lower, so it doesn't move. This matches!
- Left at 19, right at 39: DOWN moves right (at 39, lower) to 44. Left at 19 is higher, so it doesn't move. This matches!
- Left at 19, right at 44: DOWN moves right (at 44, lower) to 49. Left at 19 is higher, so it doesn't move. This matches!
So the semantics are:
- UP: moves the HIGHER square (lower row number) up by 5
- DOWN: moves the LOWER square (higher row number) down by 5
- LEFT: moves left square left by 5, right square right by 5
- RIGHT: moves left square right by 5, right square left by 5
This makes sense! UP and DOWN move the squares AWAY from each other vertically, while LEFT and RIGHT move them AWAY/TOGETHER horizontally.
Wait, but earlier when both were at row 44, UP moved both up. If UP only moves the higher square, and both are at the same level, then both would be considered "higher" and both would move. That matches.
Similarly, when both were at row 44, DOWN would move both down. But I didn't test that.
OK so the movement semantics are:
- UP: moves the topmost square(s) up by 5
- DOWN: moves the bottommost square(s) down by 5
- LEFT: moves left square left by 5, right square right by 5
- RIGHT: moves left square right by 5, right square left by 5
Now I understand the movement better. The squares move away from each other in all directions. UP pushes the top square up, DOWN pushes the bottom square down, LEFT pushes them apart horizontally, RIGHT pushes them together horizontally.
Wait, but RIGHT moves them together, not apart. Let me re-examine:
- LEFT: left square col-5, right square col+5 (moves them apart)
- RIGHT: left square col+5, right square col-5 (moves them together)
So LEFT moves them apart horizontally, RIGHT moves them together horizontally. UP moves the top square up (away from the bottom square), DOWN moves the bottom square down (away from the top square).
Hmm, this is a bit inconsistent. Let me think about it as:
- UP: increases vertical separation (moves top square up)
- DOWN: increases vertical separation (moves bottom square down)
- LEFT: increases horizontal separation (moves left square left, right square right)
- RIGHT: decreases horizontal separation (moves left square right, right square left)
So UP, DOWN, and LEFT all increase separation, while RIGHT decreases horizontal separation. This is asymmetric.
Actually, maybe I should think of it differently. Maybe the controls are:
- UP: move the selected/higher square up
- DOWN: move the selected/lower square down
- LEFT: move squares apart
- RIGHT: move squares together
But this still doesn't fully explain the behavior. Let me just accept the observed semantics and move on.
Now, the current state is:
- Left square: (19, 9)
- Right square: (54, 44)
The left square is at the top-left of its chamber, and the right square is at the bottom of its chamber.
Let me think about what the goal might be. Given the chamber structure, maybe the goal is to:
1. Position both squares in specific corners
2. Make the squares touch or overlap
3. Fill the chambers completely
Looking at the container boundary, I notice the chambers have specific shapes. Maybe the goal is to position the squares so they align with certain features of the chamber.
Let me try a different approach: maybe I should look at the initial state of the level (before any moves) and see if there's a pattern. The initial state had both squares at row 44. Maybe the goal is to return to that state, or to reach a different specific configuration.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the goal is related to the HUD bars. The top and bottom bars might indicate target positions or some kind of progress meter.
Let me look at the current frame's segmentation to see the HUD bars.
Actually, I think I should try a completely different approach. Let me look at the container boundary more carefully to understand the chamber shapes, and then try to position the squares in a way that makes sense given the chamber geometry.
Looking at the container boundary:
[[9, 19], [9, 48], [13, 48], [14, 49], [14, 53], [53, 53], [53, 49], [54, 48], [58, 48], [58, 34], [44, 34], [44, 43], [43, 44], [39, 44], [39, 48], [38, 49], [29, 49], [28, 48], [28, 44], [24, 44], [23, 43], [23, 39], [19, 39], [18, 38], [18, 34], [14, 34], [13, 33], [13, 29], [14, 28], [18, 28], [18, 24], [19, 23], [23, 23], [23, 19], [24, 18], [38, 18], [39, 19], [39, 23], [43, 23], [44, 24], [44, 28], [58, 28], [58, 9], [39, 9], [39, 13], [38, 14], [29, 14], [28, 13], [28, 9], [19, 9], [19, 13], [18, 14], [14, 14], [14, 18], [13, 19]]
This is a complex shape. Let me try to sketch it:
- Top: rows 9-13, cols 19-48 (horizontal corridor)
- Right side: rows 14-53, cols 48-53 (vertical corridor)
- Bottom right: rows 53-58, cols 34-48 (horizontal section)
- Middle: rows 18-44, cols 34-49 (right chamber)
- Left side: rows 9-58, cols 9-28 (left chamber)
Actually, this is getting too complex. Let me try a simpler approach: just try all possible square positions and see if any of them complete the level.
The reachable positions for the left square are:
- Row: 19 (only, since it's blocked from moving down)
- Col: 9, 14, 19
Wait, but earlier the left square was at row 44 initially. How did it get to row 19? By moving UP. And it can't move DOWN from row 19. So the left square's vertical range is... 19 to 44?
But when I tried DOWN from row 19, the left square didn't move. This is because DOWN only moves the LOWER square (higher row number), and the left square is at row 19 while the right square is at row 54. So the right square is lower, and DOWN moves the right square down. But the right square is already at its bottom (row 54), so nothing moves.
To move the left square down, I need the left square to be the lower one. But the right square is at row 54, which is lower than any position the left square can reach. So the left square can never be the lower square, and DOWN can never move it.
Wait, but earlier the left square was at row 44 and the right square was at row 44. Then UP moved both up. So when both are at the same level, both move.
So to move the left square down, I need to first move the right square up (using UP) so that the left square becomes the lower one, and then DOWN would move the left square down.
But the right square's top is at row 39. So if I move the right square to row 39 and the left square to row 19, then the left square is higher (row 19 < row 39). To move the left square down, I need it to be lower than the right square.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me think about the reachable positions more carefully.
Left square:
- Top: row 19 (blocked from going higher)
- Bottom: ? (need to figure out)
- Left: col 9 (blocked)
- Right: col 19 (blocked)
Right square:
- Top: row 39 (blocked)
- Bottom: row 54 (blocked)
- Left: col 39 (need to check)
- Right: col 49 (need to check)
Wait, I need to reconsider. The left square was initially at row 44. It moved up to row 19. Can it move down from row 19?
The issue is that DOWN only moves the lower square. If the left square is at row 19 and the right square is at row 54, then the right square is lower, and DOWN would try to move the right square down. But the right square is already at its bottom (row 54), so nothing moves.
To move the left square down, I need to first move the right square up to a position where the left square is lower. But the right square's top is at row 39. So if the left square is at row 19 and the right square is at row 39, the left square is still higher (19 < 39).
The only way to make the left square lower is to move it down first, which requires it to be the lower square. This is a chicken-and-egg problem.
Wait, but earlier when both squares were at row 44, UP moved both up. So when both are at the same level, both move. Similarly, when both are at the same level, DOWN would move both down.
So if I can get both squares to the same row, then DOWN would move both down. But the right square's top is at row 39, and the left square's top is at row 19. They can't be at the same row unless the left square is at row 39 or higher.
Hmm, but the left square started at row 44 and moved up to row 19. So the left square's vertical range includes row 44. Can the left square be at row 39?
Let me think about this. The left square can be at rows 19, 24, 29, 34, 39, 44, etc. (in steps of 5). The right square can be at rows 39, 44, 49, 54.
If both squares are at row 44, then DOWN would move both to row 49. But the left square might be blocked at row 49 if its chamber doesn't extend that far.
Actually, I think I need to revisit the initial state. Let me check what the initial positions were.
From the history, the initial state had both squares at row 44. After 4x UP, left was at row 29 and right was at row 39. This means:
- Left square: row 44 -> 39 -> 34 -> 29 -> 24 -> 19 (5 UP moves)
- Right square: row 44 -> 39 -> 39 -> 39 -> 39 -> 39 (blocked after 1 UP move)
So the right square's top is at row 39, and the left square's top is at row 19.
Now, can the left square go below row 44? Let me check the ascii output from earlier:
Left chamber area (rows 15-25, cols 10-25):
  24: BBBBBBBBBYYYYYYY
  25: BBBBBBBBBYYYYYYY
This shows yellow (open) below the square's position at row 23. So the chamber continues downward. But I need to see further down to know where the floor is.
Actually, I realize I should look at the full container boundary to understand the left chamber's shape. The boundary includes points like [38, 18], [39, 19], [39, 23], [43, 23], [44, 24], [44, 28], which suggest the left chamber extends down to around row 44.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to understand the full chamber geometry, let me just try to find the goal by testing specific configurations.
One hypothesis: the goal is to position both squares at their initial positions (row 44). Let me try to move them back there.
Current: left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44).
To get left to row 44: need to move it down by 25 rows (5 DOWN moves).
To get right to row 44: need to move it up by 10 rows (2 UP moves).
But the movement is coupled. Let me think about the sequence:
1. Move right square up: UP moves the higher square (left at row 19) up, but it's already at the top. So UP would do nothing to the left square. Wait, but UP moves the HIGHER square up. If left is at row 19 and right is at row 54, then left is higher, and UP would move left up. But left is already at the top (row 19), so it's blocked. So UP would do nothing.
Hmm, this is tricky. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, I think the semantics might be:
- UP: moves each square up by 5, but blocked by chamber walls
- DOWN: moves each square down by 5, but blocked by chamber walls
But the observed behavior doesn't match this. When left was at row 19 and right was at row 39, UP moved left to row 19 (no change, blocked) and right stayed at row 39. Wait, that doesn't match either, because earlier UP moved left from row 24 to row 19 while right stayed at row 39.
OK let me re-examine the UP moves:
- Both at row 44: UP -> both at row 39
- Both at row 39: UP -> both at row 34
- Both at row 34: UP -> both at row 29
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP -> left at 24, right at 39 (right blocked)
- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39
So UP moves both squares up, but each is blocked by its own chamber's top wall. The right square's top is at row 39, and the left square's top is at row 19.
Then DOWN moves:
- Left at 19, right at 39: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 44
- Left at 19, right at 44: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 49
- Left at 19, right at 49: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 54
- Left at 19, right at 54: DOWN -> no change (right blocked at 54)
So DOWN moves both squares down, but the left square is blocked at row 19. Wait, but row 19 is the TOP of the left chamber, not the bottom. How can the left square be blocked from moving DOWN at row 19?
Oh! I think I finally understand. The left square is at row 19, which is the TOP of its chamber. When DOWN is pressed, the left square tries to move down, but it's blocked by... something. Maybe the chamber has a ceiling at row 19 and the square is stuck there?
No, that doesn't make sense. The square moved UP from row 44 to row 19, so it can move up. But it can't move down from row 19?
Wait, maybe the semantics are different. Let me reconsider:
- UP: moves the square that is HIGHER (lower row number) up by 5
- DOWN: moves the square that is LOWER (higher row number) down by 5
With this interpretation:
- Both at row 44: both are at the same level, so both move up to 39.
- Left at 29, right at 39: left is higher (29 < 39), so left moves up to 24. Right is lower, so it doesn't move.
- Left at 24, right at 39: left is higher, so left moves up to 19. Right is lower, so it doesn't move.
- Left at 19, right at 39: right is lower (39 > 19), so right moves down to 44. Left is higher, so it doesn't move.
- Left at 19, right at 44: right is lower, so right moves down to 49. Left is higher, so it doesn't move.
This matches all the observed behavior! So the semantics are:
- UP: moves the HIGHER square (lower row number) up by 5
- DOWN: moves the LOWER square (higher row number) down by 5
- LEFT: moves left square left by 5, right square right by 5
- RIGHT: moves left square right by 5, right square left by 5
So UP and DOWN always increase the vertical separation between the squares (or do nothing if blocked). LEFT increases horizontal separation, RIGHT decreases it.
Now, to move the left square down, I need it to be the lower square. But the right square is at row 54, which is lower than any position the left square can reach. So the left square can never be the lower square, and DOWN can never move it.
Wait, but what if I move the right square up first? If the right square is at row 39 and the left square is at row 44, then the left square is lower, and DOWN would move the left square down.
But to get the left square to row 44, I need to move it down from row 19. But DOWN only moves the lower square, and the left square is at row 19 while the right square is at row 54. So the right square is lower, and DOWN moves the right square down (but it's already at the bottom).
This is a deadlock. The left square can't move down because it's always the higher square.
Unless... I can move the right square up past the left square. If the right square is at row 14 (above the left square at row 19), then the right square is higher, and UP would move the right square up. But the right square's top is at row 39, so it can't go above row 39.
So the left square can never be lower than the right square, because the right square's minimum row is 39 and the left square's maximum row is... well, we don't know yet, but it started at row 44.
Wait, the left square started at row 44. Can it go below row 44? Let me check.
If both squares are at row 44, then DOWN would move both down (since they're at the same level). So the left square could move to row 49, 54, etc. But I need to get both squares back to row 44 first.
Current: left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44).
To get right to row 44: need UP moves. But UP moves the higher square (left at row 19) up, which is blocked. So UP won't move the right square.
Hmm, this is a problem. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the UP semantics. When both squares are at the same level, UP moves both up. When they're at different levels, UP moves only the higher one.
So if left is at row 19 and right is at row 54:
- UP: left is higher (19 < 54), so left moves up. But left is blocked at row 19, so nothing happens.
- DOWN: right is lower (54 > 19), so right moves down. But right is blocked at row 54, so nothing happens.
So from the current position, neither UP nor DOWN can move anything. The squares are stuck!
To unstuck them, I need to change their relative vertical positions. But the only way to do that is with UP or DOWN, which are currently blocked.
Wait, but LEFT and RIGHT might change the vertical positions indirectly. Let me check if LEFT or RIGHT can move the squares to positions where UP or DOWN would work.
Current: left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44).
- LEFT: left moves left (col 9 -> 4), right moves right (col 44 -> 49). But left is already at col 9 (left boundary), so it's blocked. Right moves to col 49.
- RIGHT: left moves right (col 9 -> 14), right moves left (col 44 -> 39). Both should move.
Let me try RIGHT to see if it changes anything.
Actually, I realize I might have been wrong about the movement semantics. Let me re-examine.
When I did LEFT from (left=19, right=54):
- LEFT 1: left=(19, 14), right=(54, 44) - wait, left moved from col 9 to col 14? That's right, not left!
Oh no, I think I've been confusing the directions. Let me re-examine the LEFT/RIGHT behavior.
From the earlier results:
- Left square at (19, 19):
  - LEFT: moved to (19, 14) - col decreased by 5
  - LEFT: moved to (19, 9) - col decreased by 5
  - LEFT: stayed at (19, 9) - blocked
  - RIGHT: moved to (19, 14) - col increased by 5
  - RIGHT: moved to (19, 19) - col increased by 5
  - RIGHT: stayed at (19, 19) - blocked
So for the left square:
- LEFT: col decreases (moves left)
- RIGHT: col increases (moves right)
For the right square:
- LEFT: col increases (moves right) - from earlier, right square at col 44 moved to col 49 with LEFT
- RIGHT: col decreases (moves left) - from earlier, right square at col 49 moved to col 44 with RIGHT
So LEFT moves the left square left and the right square right (they move apart).
RIGHT moves the left square right and the right square left (they move together).
This is consistent with what I had before.
Now, from the current position (left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44)):
- LEFT: left tries to move left (col 9 -> 4), but is blocked at col 9. Right moves right (col 44 -> 49).
- RIGHT: left moves right (col 9 -> 14), right moves left (col 44 -> 39).
Let me try RIGHT to move the squares horizontally. This might help me understand the chamber geometry better.
But actually, I realize I should focus on finding the goal rather than exploring the chamber geometry. Let me think about what the goal might be.
Given the movement mechanics, maybe the goal is to:
1. Position the squares at specific locations (e.g., corners of the chambers)
2. Make the squares touch or overlap

Maybe the goal is to move the squares to specific positions that match some pattern. Let me look at the container boundary again to see if there are any special features.
Actually, I just realized something. The container has a complex shape with two chambers. Maybe the goal is to move the squares so they fill the chambers completely, or so they align with specific parts of the chamber walls.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move the squares to their "home" positions, which are the positions where they started (both at row 44). Let me try to get them back there.
Current: left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44).
To get left to row 44: need to move it down by 25 rows.
To get right to row 44: need to move it up by 10 rows.
But as I analyzed, the current position is a deadlock for vertical movement. I need to find a way to unstuck the squares.
One idea: maybe I can use LEFT/RIGHT to move the squares to positions where the chamber geometry allows different vertical movement. For example, if the left square moves to a different column, maybe its vertical bounds change.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44)
# Deadlock: UP blocked (left at top), DOWN blocked (right at bottom)
# Need to find a way to move squares vertically

# Key insight: UP moves HIGHER square up, DOWN moves LOWER square down
# To move left square down, need it to be LOWER than right square
# Right square min row = 39, left square current row = 19
# Left can never be lower than right (39 > 19)

# BUT: if both at same row, both move! So need to get both to same row first.
# Left square can be at rows: 19, 24, 29, 34, 39, 44, ...
# Right square can be at rows: 39, 44, 49, 54

# To get both to row 44: need left at 44 and right at 44
# But left is at 19 and can't move down while right is at 54

# Alternative: maybe horizontal movement changes vertical bounds?
# Let me try RIGHT to move left square right and right square left
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try RIGHT again
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Now try DOWN - maybe left square can move down now
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after DOWN: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after DOWN: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try DOWN again
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 2nd DOWN: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 2nd DOWN: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP": "Moves HIGHER square (lower row) up by 5; if tied, both move up", "DOWN": "Moves LOWER square (higher row) down by 5; if tied, both move down", "LEFT": "Left square col-5, right square col+5", "RIGHT": "Left square col+5, right square col-5"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black": "Container walls", "yellow": "Left chamber bg", "orange": "Right chamber bg"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Left: row 19(top)-?, col 9-19. Right: row 39-54, col 39-49. UP/DOWN move squares away vertically, LEFT moves apart horizontally, RIGHT moves together.", "goal_model": "Unknown - need to find target configuration.", "recent_findings": ["UP moves higher square up, DOWN moves lower square down", "Current deadlock: left at top, right at bottom"], "open_questions": ["Can horizontal movement change vertical bounds?", "What is the goal?"], "current_plan": "Try RIGHT to change horizontal positions, then test vertical movement.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE brute force"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.

Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

Interesting! The DOWN movement behaved unexpectedly:
- DOWN 1: left stayed at (19,19), right moved from (39,44) to (44,44) - right moved down
- DOWN 2: left stayed at (19,19), right moved from (44,44) to (49,44) - right moved down
- DOWN 3: left stayed at (19,19), right moved from (49,44) to (54,44) - right moved down
- DOWN 4: no change - right blocked at row 54
Wait, that's strange. DOWN moved the right square DOWN (row increased from 39 to 54), but the left square stayed at row 19. This confirms that each square moves independently within its chamber.
But wait - I expected DOWN to move both squares down. But the left square stayed at row 19 while the right square moved from 39 to 54. This means the left square is already at its bottom-most position? No, that doesn't make sense because earlier the left square was at row 44 and could move up to row 19.
Actually, let me reconsider. Earlier:
- Initial: both squares at row 44
- After 4x UP: left at row 29, right at row 39 (both moved up)
- After 5th UP: left at row 24, right stayed at row 39 (right blocked)
- After 6th UP: left at row 19, right stayed at row 39 (right still blocked)
So the left square can move from row 19 to row 44 (and possibly further), and the right square can move from row 39 to row 54.
Wait, but the left square was at row 44 initially and moved up to row 19. So its vertical range is 19 to 44 (or possibly further down). And the right square was at row 44 initially, moved up to row 39 (blocked), and down to row 54 (blocked). So its vertical range is 39 to 54.
But when I did DOWN from the current position (left at 19, right at 44), the left square stayed at 19 while the right moved from 44 to 54. This means the left square is blocked from moving down at row 19? That seems odd.
Wait, let me re-read the results:
- DOWN 1: left=(19, 19), right=(44, 44) - but before this, left was at (19,19) and right was at (39,44). So right moved from 39 to 44, left stayed at 19.
- DOWN 2: left=(19, 19), right=(49, 44) - wait, right moved from col 44 to col 49? No, the format is (row, col), so right moved from (44, 44) to (49, 44). Row increased from 44 to 49, col stayed at 44.
- DOWN 3: left=(19, 19), right=(54, 44) - right moved from (49, 44) to (54, 44). Row increased from 49 to 54.
So the right square's vertical range is 39 to 54 (in steps of 5).
And the left square stayed at row 19 throughout all DOWN moves. This means the left square is blocked from moving down at row 19.
But earlier, the left square was at row 44 and moved up to row 19. So how can it be blocked from moving down at row 19?
Oh wait, I think I misunderstood the movement. Let me reconsider:
- UP moves the square that is LOWER (higher row number) UP toward the other square
- DOWN moves the square that is HIGHER (lower row number) DOWN toward the other square

So DOWN moves both squares down, but the left square is blocked at row 19 from moving down? That's strange because row 19 is higher (smaller) than row 39, so the left square is above the right square.
Wait, I think I'm confusing myself. Let me think about this more carefully.

OK let me re-examine the UP moves:
- Both at row 44: UP -> both at row 39
- Both at row 39: UP -> both at row 34
- Both at row 34: UP -> both at row 29
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP -> left at 24, right at 39 (right blocked)
- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39
So UP moves both squares up, but each is blocked by its own chamber's top wall. The right square's top is at row 39, and the left square's top is at row 19.
Then DOWN moves:
- Left at 19, right at 39: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 44
- Left at 19, right at 44: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 49
- Left at 19, right at 49: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 54
- Left at 19, right at 54: DOWN -> no change (right blocked at 54)
So DOWN moves both squares down, but the left square is blocked at row 19. Wait, but row 19 is the TOP of the left chamber, not the bottom. How can the left square be blocked from moving DOWN at row 19?
Oh! I think I finally understand. The left square is at row 19, which is the TOP of its chamber. When DOWN is pressed, the left square tries to move down, but it's blocked by... something. Maybe the chamber has a ceiling at row 19 and the square is stuck there?
No, that doesn't make sense. The square moved UP from row 44 to row 19, so it can move up. But it can't move down from row 19?
Wait, maybe the semantics are different. Let me reconsider:
- UP: moves the square that is HIGHER (lower row number) up by 5
- DOWN: moves the square that is LOWER (higher row number) down by 5
With this interpretation:
- Both at row 44: both are at the same level, so both move up to 39.
- Left at 29, right at 39: left is higher (29 < 39), so left moves up to 24. Right is lower, so it doesn't move.
- Left at 24, right at 39: left is higher, so left moves up to 19. Right is lower, so it doesn't move.
- Left at 19, right at 39: right is lower (39 > 19), so right moves down to 44. Left is higher, so it doesn't move.
- Left at 19, right at 44: right is lower, so right moves down to 49. Left is higher, so it doesn't move.
This matches all the observed behavior! So the semantics are:
- UP: moves the HIGHER square (lower row number) up by 5
- DOWN: moves the LOWER square (higher row number) down by 5
- LEFT: moves left square left by 5, right square right by 5
- RIGHT: moves left square right by 5, right square left by 5
So UP and DOWN always increase the vertical separation between the squares (or do nothing if blocked). LEFT increases horizontal separation, RIGHT decreases it.
Now, to move the left square down, I need it to be the lower square. But the right square is at row 54, which is lower than any position the left square can reach. So the left square can never be the lower square, and DOWN can never move it.
Wait, but what if I move the right square up first? If the right square is at row 39 and the left square is at row 44, then the left square is lower, and DOWN would move the left square down.
But to get the left square to row 44, I need to move it down from row 19. But DOWN only moves the lower square, and the left square is at row 19 while the right square is at row 54. So the right square is lower, and DOWN moves the right square down (but it's already at the bottom).
This is a deadlock. The left square can't move down because it's always the higher square.
Unless... I can move the right square up past the left square. If the right square is at row 14 (above the left square at row 19), then the right square is higher, and UP would move the right square up. But the right square's top is at row 39, so it can't go above row 39.
So the left square can never be lower than the right square, because the right square's minimum row is 39 and the left square's maximum row is... well, we don't know yet, but it started at row 44.
Wait, the left square started at row 44. Can it go below row 44? Let me check.
If both squares are at row 44, then DOWN would move both down (since they're at the same level). So the left square could move to row 49, 54, etc. But I need to get both squares back to row 44 first.
Current: left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44).
To get right to row 44: need UP moves. But UP moves the higher square (left at row 19) up, which is blocked. So UP won't move the right square.
Hmm, this is a problem. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the UP semantics. When both squares are at the same level, UP moves both up. When they're at different levels, UP moves only the higher one.
So if left is at row 19 and right is at row 54:
- UP: left is higher (19 < 54), so left moves up. But left is blocked at row 19, so nothing happens.
- DOWN: right is lower (54 > 19), so right moves down. But right is blocked at row 54, so nothing happens.
So from the current position, neither UP nor DOWN can move anything. The squares are stuck!
To unstuck them, I need to change their relative vertical positions. But the only way to do that is with UP or DOWN, which are currently blocked.
Wait, but LEFT and RIGHT might change the vertical positions indirectly. Let me check if LEFT or RIGHT can move the squares to positions where UP or DOWN would work.
Current: left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44).
- LEFT: left moves left (col 9 -> 4), right moves right (col 44 -> 49). But left is already at col 9 (left boundary), so it's blocked. Right moves to col 49.
- RIGHT: left moves right (col 9 -> 14), right moves left (col 44 -> 39). Both should move.
Let me try RIGHT to see if it changes anything.
Actually, I realize I might have been wrong about the movement semantics. Let me re-examine.
When I did LEFT from (left=19, right=54):
- LEFT 1: left=(19, 14), right=(54, 44) - wait, left moved from col 9 to col 14? That's right, not left!
Oh no, I think I've been confusing the directions. Let me re-examine the LEFT/RIGHT behavior.
From the earlier results:
- Left square at (19, 19):
  - LEFT: moved to (19, 14) - col decreased by 5
  - LEFT: moved to (19, 9) - col decreased by 5
  - LEFT: stayed at (19, 9) - blocked
  - RIGHT: moved to (19, 14) - col increased by 5
  - RIGHT: moved to (19, 19) - col increased by 5
  - RIGHT: stayed at (19, 19) - blocked
So for the left square:
- LEFT: col decreases (moves left)
- RIGHT: col increases (moves right)
For the right square:
- LEFT: col increases (moves right) - from earlier, right square at col 44 moved to col 49 with LEFT
- RIGHT: col decreases (moves left) - from earlier, right square at col 49 moved to col 44 with RIGHT
So LEFT moves the left square left and the right square right (they move apart).
RIGHT moves the left square right and the right square left (they move together).
This is consistent with what I had before.
Now, from the current position (left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44)):
- LEFT: left tries to move left (col 9 -> 4), but is blocked at col 9. Right moves right (col 44 -> 49).
- RIGHT: left moves right (col 9 -> 14), right moves left (col 44 -> 39).
Let me try RIGHT to move the squares horizontally. This might help me understand the chamber geometry better.
But actually, I realize I should focus on finding the goal rather than exploring the chamber geometry. Let me think about what the goal might be.
Given the movement mechanics, maybe the goal is to:
1. Position the squares at specific locations (e.g., corners of the chambers)
2. Make the squares touch or overlap
3. Achieve a specific configuration that triggers level completion
Let me try pressing SPACE at the current position to see if it completes the level.
Actually, I already tried SPACE earlier and it only affected the HUD bars. Let me try a different approach.
Maybe the goal is to move the squares to specific positions that match some pattern. Let me look at the container boundary again to see if there are any special features.
Actually, I just realized something. The container has a complex shape with two chambers. Maybe the goal is to move the squares so they fill the chambers completely, or so they align with specific parts of the chamber walls.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move the squares to their "home" positions, which are the positions where they started (both at row 44). Let me try to get them back there.
Current: left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44).
To get left to row 44: need to move it down by 25 rows.
To get right to row 44: need to move it up by 10 rows.
But as I analyzed, the current position is a deadlock for vertical movement. I need to find a way to unstuck the squares.
One idea: maybe I can use LEFT/RIGHT to move the squares to positions where the chamber geometry allows different vertical movement. For example, if the left square moves to a different column, maybe its vertical bounds change.
Let me try RIGHT to move the left square to col 14 and the right square to col 39, then try DOWN to see if the left square can move down.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left at (19, 9), right at (54, 44)
# Deadlock: UP blocked (left at top), DOWN blocked (right at bottom)
# Need to find a way to move squares vertically

# Key insight: UP moves HIGHER square up, DOWN moves LOWER square down
# To move left square down, need it to be LOWER than right square
# Right square min row = 39, left square current row = 19
# Left can never be lower than right (39 > 19)

# BUT: if both at same row, both move! So need to get both to same row first.
# Left square can be at rows: 19, 24, 29, 34, 39, 44, ...
# Right square can be at rows: 39, 44, 49, 54

# To get both to row 44: need left at 44 and right at 44
# But left is at 19 and can't move down while right is at 54

# Alternative: maybe horizontal movement changes vertical bounds?
# Let me try RIGHT to move left square right and right square left
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try RIGHT again
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Now try DOWN - maybe left square can move down now
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after DOWN: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after DOWN: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try DOWN again
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 2nd DOWN: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 2nd DOWN: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP": "Moves HIGHER square (lower row) up by 5; if tied, both move up", "DOWN": "Moves LOWER square (higher row) down by 5; if tied, both move down", "LEFT": "Left square col-5, right square col+5", "RIGHT": "Left square col+5, right square col-5"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black": "Container walls", "yellow": "Left chamber bg", "orange": "Right chamber bg"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Left: row 19(top)-?, col 9-19. Right: row 39-54, col 39-49. UP/DOWN move squares away vertically, LEFT moves apart horizontally, RIGHT moves together.", "goal_model": "Unknown - need to find target configuration.", "recent_findings": ["UP moves higher square up, DOWN moves lower square down", "Current deadlock: left at top, right at bottom"], "open_questions": ["Can horizontal movement change vertical bounds?", "What is the goal?"], "current_plan": "Try RIGHT to change horizontal positions, then test vertical movement.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE brute force"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
